title: The Operational Data Analytics (ODA) for Service Speed Design
abstract: We develop the operational data analytics (ODA) framework for the classical service design problem ofG/G/c/ksystems. The customer arrival rate is unknown. Instead, some historical data of interarrival times are collected. The data-integration model, specifying the mapping from the arrival data to the service rate, is formulated based on the time-scaling property of the stochastic service process. Validating the data-integration model against the long-run average service reward leads to a uniformly optimal service rate for any given sample size. We further derive the ODA-predicted reward function based on the data-integration model, which gives a consistent estimate of the underlying reward function. Our numerical experiments show that the ODA framework can lead to an efficient design of service rate and service capacity, which is insensitive to model specification. The ODA solution exhibits superior performance compared with the conventional estimation-and-then-optimization solutions in the small sample regime.

author.Qi Feng: Mitchell E. Daniels, Jr. School of Business, Purdue University, West Lafayette, Indiana 47907
author.Zhibin Jiang: Antai School of Economics and Management, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, China
author.J. George Shanthikumar: Mitchell E. Daniels, Jr. School of Business, Purdue University, West Lafayette, Indiana 47907
author.Yang Yang: Antai School of Economics and Management, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, China

article.1. Introduction: Queuing models have been the building blocks of many operating systems. Most early studies of multiserver queues with finite capacity and finite buffers have been widely applied to manufacturing systems (Buzacott and Shanthikumar 1993). Service or production speed design is a classical problem in manufacturing, as it is a key determinant of throughput and productivity. The speed adjustment is often achieved through changing the pace of the transfer lines and load balancing. In the service industry, service speed can greatly impact customer satisfaction (e.g.,Zhou et al. 2014). For example, in retail stores, customers often appreciate quick checkout, and long queues can induce reduced store visits (e.g.,Li et al. 2016). In the entertainment industry, service time is associated with significant effort, which often determines the perceived quality (e.g.,Wang et al. 2022). Queuing models have also been applied to studying online platforms, including on-demand computing, on-demand delivery (e.g., food, ride, products), on-demand work (e.g., freelance), and knowledge sharing. In many situations, the manager has some lever to control the service speed through the format of service offering (e.g., add-on service), employee training, and resource planning. The knowledge of service offerings, employee capability, and equipment efficiency is relatively easy to acquire. In contrast, the customer arrival pattern can be highly uncertain for new service offerings or may be changing with market conditions. There may be a lack of complete characterization of the customer arrival process because of insufficient data relevant to the future business environment (e.g.,Miranda 2021,Lawton 2023). Instead, decisions must be made based on past experience and limited arrival data. We consider the service design problem that accounts for the reward obtained from customer service completion, the cost of maintaining the chosen service speed, and the penalty for keeping customers waiting. The customers’ arrival rate is unknown, and only limited data of past arrivals are available. We develop an operational data analytics (ODA) framework for the data-integrated service design of multiserver systems with finite capacity and finite buffers. As a major departure from the existing data-driven or data-integration approaches, the unique feature of the ODA framework lies in identifying thedirectmapping from the observed data to an implementable decision. Specifically, the ODA framework consists of two pillars, adata-integration modeland avalidation model. The data-integration model, consisting of a class of operational statistics, specifies how the eventual service speed decision should be related to the observed data of interarrival time. Recognizing the fact that the key service measures, including steady-state distribution, customer joining probability, and queue length, are not affected when the time dimension is rescaled, the mean service time must be a homogenous function of the observed interarrival times. Thus, the decision-making problem boils down to choosing an operational statistic from the class of homogenous functions. In formulating the validation model, we explicitly compute the expected long-run average reward when a certain operational statistic is implemented. In theory, one cannot compute the validation model, as the true customer arrival rate is unknown. However, given the family of interarrival time distribution, we show that the optimal operational statistic can be derived without knowing the exact arrival rate. In other words, we obtain a uniformly optimal decision with respect to any given sample size. We first demonstrate the ODA framework for theM/M/c/ksystems (with exponentially distributed interarrival time and service time,cservers, and(k−c)buffer) and then generalize the results to generalG/G/c/ksystems (with generally distributed interarrival time and service time,cservers, and(k−c)buffers). With the explicit forms of the measures for theM/M/c/ksystems, we obtain clear insights into the ODA solution. In this case, the expected reward is independent of the individual observations whenever the sample average equals one. The resulting optimal service rate, called the base operational statistic, can be easily computed with only the information of the reward and cost parameters. The ODA solution for any sample can then be obtained by scaling the base operational statistic with the sample average. For the general arrival process, however, the service rate decision can be different even when sample averages of the interarrival times are the same. In other words, the system design is more sensitive to the observed data for general queuing systems than forM/M/c/ksystems. We prove that the ODA solution is uniformly optimal for any given sample size, and it is asymptotically optimal with an optimality gap bounded by1/n(wherenis the sample size). We further show that the ODA framework has a Bayesian representation. Specifically, implementing any operational statistic from the homogenous data-integration model is equivalent to updating the arrival rate distribution with data, starting from an uninformative prior. This observation allows us to derive an estimate of the reward function for any service rate, service capacity, and buffer size. We show that this estimate is consistent and exhibits superior estimation accuracy with small samples. The Bayesian representation also allows for the accommodation of additional arrival knowledge into the ODA-predicted reward. Using the ODA-predicted reward, we can optimize other service design parameters (like the service capacity) and derive efficient data-integrated decisions. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we review the related literature to articulate the contribution of our work. We use theM/M/c/ksystem as the base model to describe the key elements of the ODA framework inSection 3. InSection 4, we generalize the analysis toG/G/c/ksystems and develop the ODA prediction-and-then-optimization framework inSection 5. The study is concluded inSection 6, where we discuss potential directions for future research. The proofs of formal results and some detailed analyses are relegated to online appendices.
article.2. Literature Review: G/G/c/kqueues have been widely applied to understand the process flows in various contexts, including, most notably, manufacturing systems, customer services systems, call centers, and healthcare systems. Control of service speed is a central consideration in these applications. In manufacturing systems, the speed of the server is a key configuration that determines process productivity and throughput (see the classical references byBuzacott and Shanthikumar 1993,Shanthikumar et al. 2007). Recent studies have discussed how the service speed decision can be affected by pricing, quality, and flexibility measures (e.g.,Lederer and Li 1997,Hall and Porteus 2000,Plambeck and Zenios 2003,Chen and Frank 2004,Kostami and Rajagopalan 2014). In service supply chains, service speed is a key determinant of customer satisfaction, strategic customer behavior, and competitiveness (e.g.,Hassin and Haviv 2003,Burnetas 2022,Lin et al. 2023). In these applications, queuing models constitute building blocks in monopoly settings (e.g.,Gilbert and Weng 1998,Debo et al. 2008,Kumar and Randhawa 2010,Anand et al. 2011,Geng et al. 2015) and multifirm settings (e.g.,Kalai et al. 1992,Allon and Federgruen 2007,Li et al. 2012,Yang et al. 2014). An extensively analyzed service system is the call center (see the survey byAksin et al. 2007) in which service speed can greatly impact customer satisfaction measured by either the waiting time or the bulking rate. Queuing models are also widely adopted to analyze healthcare systems for, for example, organ allocation (e.g.,Su and Zenios 2006,Akan et al. 2012), discharging schedule (e.g.,Dai and Shi 2017,Shi et al. 2021a), staffing decision (e.g.,Yom-Tov and Mandelbaum 2014), diversion policy (e.g.,Xu and Chan 2016), reimbursement policy (Guo et al. 2019), healthcare service schedule (e.g.,Baron et al. 2017,Liu et al. 2023), and test design (e.g.,Shi et al. 2021b). Most of the studies applying queuing models assume full characterization of the stochastic system under consideration and focus on deriving the structural properties of the optimal decisions. In reality, however, the decision maker may not have enough knowledge of the customer arrival process (Mehrotra et al. 2010,Taylor 2012). To address this lack of knowledge, historical data are often used to estimate the system parameters and calibrate the model. The applications are wide, and we name a few recent examples of predictive modeling.Senderovich et al. (2015)combine colored Petri nets and folding operations of projection to abstract complex scheduling processes.van der Mei and Bhulai (2022)approximate vital performance metrics (e.g., waiting time) of queuing models based on genetic algorithms. Other related work includesRozinat et al. (2009),Chao et al. (2018),Mesabbah et al. (2019), andCamargo et al. (2020). The majority of the work on data-driven queuing design falls into the estimation-and-then-optimization (ETO) framework (de Véricourt and Perakis 2020). The unknown characterizations of the system are estimated or predicted using historical data through various techniques. Machine learning (e.g.,Mehrbod et al. 2018,Zhu et al. 2022,Chen et al. 2023,Jiang et al. 2023) and discrete event simulation (Jónasson et al. 2017,Kim et al. 2018) are often applied. Then, the estimated or predicted model is embedded into the optimization problem, and the decisions are analyzed using a mixed-integer program (e.g.,Zhu et al. 2022), approximate dynamic program (e.g.,Moallemi et al. 2008), or asymptotic analysis (e.g.,Bassamboo and Zeevi 2009). Several authors propose ways to directly integrate data into the decision through empirical risk minimization and compare the resulting solution against the ETO solution.Elmachtoub et al. (2023)examine the sample-average approximation against the ETO solution under model misidentification.Hu et al. (2022)evaluate the induced empirical risk minimization for the smart-predict-then-optimize solution proposed byElmachtoub and Grigas (2022). When data are collected over time and decisions are made sequentially, there is a trade-off between exploration and exploitation. Several studies apply the bandit algorithm and perform regret analysis (Krishnasamy et al. 2016,Hsu et al. 2022) on queuing systems with stochastic characterizations. Bayesian approaches are applied to learn queuing systems with uncertain service times (e.g.,Bimpikis and Markakis 2019,Shah et al. 2020) to match tasks with resources. As a major departure from this line of work, the ODA solution derived in our study seeks the direct linkage between the data and the ultimate decision to be implemented, omitting the need for parameter estimation or model calibration. Most importantly, this direct linkage is established through exploring the structure of the underlying decision-making problem, setting the foundation for establishing uniform optimality of the ODA solution. The validation model accounts for the fact that the decision is a mapping of random realizations (i.e., data), leading to an efficient and robust solution against the sampling risk. The ODA framework (Feng and Shanthikumar 2022) has been applied to estimate the consumer choice model (Feng et al. 2022) and to optimize the pricing-newsvendor model (Chu et al. 2024). This is the first work analyzing the ODA approach for service design problems, which reveals a very different structure and property from other problems. Moreover, we develop the ODA-predict-and-optimization framework, which allows for the evaluation of different service design parameters. When the full characterization of the queuing system is not available, another popular approach is to specify an uncertainty set of potential models (e.g.,Bandi et al. 2015,Bren and Saghafian 2019,Mandelbaum et al. 2020) and apply robust optimization. For example,Bertsimas et al. (2017)identify an uncertainty set of arrival time and service time densities based on hypothesis testing and then evaluate the worst case for system waiting time in aG/G/1queue.Bandi et al. (2015)model polyhedral uncertainty sets of the arrival and service time and obtain closed-form upper bounds of the system time in aG/G/cqueue.Whitt and You (2019)construct a deterministic uncertainty set for the net input process, and develop a robust queuing algorithm to approximate the time-varying workload in aGt/Gt/1queue. A key difference between the ODA approach and robust optimization lies in the fact that the latter aims toward optimizing the worst-case performance, whereas the former focuses on optimizing the ultimate system performance. Therefore, the solution to a robust problem is sensitive to the specification of the uncertainty set. As shown in our analysis inSection 5.2, the ODA solution is robust to the specified range of the unknown parameter.
article.3. The ODA Framework forM/M/c/kService S: In this section, we present the base model ofM/M/c/ksystems. TheM/M/c/ksystem is worth a separate study as it has been widely applied in the operations literature (see, e.g.,Ata and Shneorson 2006,Lu et al. 2009,Anand et al. 2011,Yom-Tov and Mandelbaum 2014,Wang et al. 2022). The explicit forms of the measures inM/M/c/kqueues allow us to derive clear insights into the ODA approach. InSection 3.1, we describe the problem of choosing the optimal service speed. InSection 3.2, we develop the ODA formulation for data integration and derive the ODA solution. 3.1. Problem Description We consider anM/M/c/kservice system with exponentially distributed interarrival time and service time, operatingcservers to accommodate up tokcustomers at any time withc≤k. We shall assume thatkis finite. This assumption is practical because the resources needed for maintaining waiting customers are generally limited; though, with exponentially distributed service times, the optimal service design would lead to a stable queuing system even with an infinitek. Therefore, the buffer size isk−c, which is the maximum number of customers waiting for service. An arriving customer who findskcustomers in the system would not be able to join the service. The customers arrive to the system according to a Poisson process with an unknown arrival rateΛT. In other words, the interarrival timeXhas densityfX(x|Λ)=Λe−ΛxwithΛ=ΛTunknown. Only a sample of interarrival timesX={X1,X2,…,Xn}is observed. We assume no censoring in the observation. That is, even if an arrival does not enter service, the arrival time is observed. The design parameter for the queuing system is the service rateμ. Defineρ=Λ/μ. It is standard to derive the effective arrival rate as (we provide the details in Online Appendix B for the sake of completeness) A service price ofpis charged, and a service cost ofbis paid for each unit of time in service. The system also incurs a goodwill loss ofwfor each unit of time for customer waiting. The long-run average service reward is In reality, there may be a cost charged for each number of servers installed in the system. This cost, however, is generally independent of the arrival rate and service rate, and it is without loss of generality to normalize it to zero in our analysis. The reward functionψ, though neither concave nor convex in general, reveals an interesting property. In particular, when both the arrival rate and the service rate are scaled by the same factor, the long-run average reward stays unchanged. This property is stated in the next lemma. The long-run average rewardψ(Λ,μ)is a homogenous function of degree zero; that is,ψ(ηΛ,ημ)=ψ(Λ,μ)for anyη>0. Our analysis focuses primarily on the design of service speed, determined byμ(though inSection 5, we will also discuss the choice of service capacityc). Based on the property of the long-run average reward function, we can identify the structure of the optimal service rateμ*(Λ)∈argmax{ψ(Λ,μ),μ≥0}in the next proposition. This structure turns out to be critical to formulating the data-integration model in the next section. (Properties ofM/M/c/kService Speed).For anyμ*(Λ)=argmax{ψ(Λ,μ),μ≥0},there exists aμ*(1)∈argmax{ψ(1,μ),μ≥0}such thatμ*(Λ)=Λμ*(1)andψ(Λ,μ*(Λ))=ψ(1,μ*(1)). When the true arrival rateΛTis known, the decision maker simply implementsμ*(ΛT)and obtains the theoretical optimal reward of Because the true arrival rateΛTis unknown, we would not know the theoretically optimal solutionμ*(ΛT). Instead, we have collected historical data of interarrival timesX={X1,X2,…,Xn}and need to make a decision based on the data. The conventional approach, the so-calledestimation-and-then-optimizationapproach, is to estimate the mean arrival time by taking the sample average, the minimum variance unbiased estimator. Thus, the estimated arrival rate (that minimizes the empirical loss functionL(Λ^,X)=1/n∑i=1n(1/Λ^−Xi)2based on the observed data is Using this estimator to replace the true arrival rate, we compute the estimated reward as Optimizing the service rate from the estimated reward gives the service rate When the sample sizenis small, the estimator in (5) may not account for the sample risk. As an alternative,Stein (1981)proposes the following unbiased risk estimator: Our task is to develop a data-integrated solution that not only outperforms the ETO and ETO-S solutions but also demonstrates superior performance with small samples. 3.2. The ODA Service Design forM/M/c/kSystems In this section, we develop the ODA framework for theM/M/c/kservice system. There are two pillars of this framework, adata-integrationmodel that identifies the potential mapping from the data to the decision and avalidationmodel that evaluates the decision against the ultimate performance measure. Unlike the estimation-and-then-optimization approach, which uses data to estimate the unknown parameter as a surrogate to the true parameter, the ODA data-integration model focuses on characterizing the relationship between the data and the decision based on the structure of the optimization problem. Specifically, fromProposition 1, if the arrival rate is scaled by some factor, then we should expect that the optimal service rate be scaled by the same factor. Thus, we can define the following data-integration model: Each element inGis a function that maps the observed dataxto a nonnegative number, which is the decision, the service rate. Thus,g∈Gis a statistic (i.e., a function of data), and it is termed anoperational statisticbecause it is an implementable decision. Any statistic withinGis an order-negative-one homogenous function. We shall note thatμETOandμETO-Sderived in the previous subsection satisfy the condition forG. That is, the estimation-and-then-optimization solutions are elements of the set of operational statisticsG. To appropriately choose a solution from the setG, we need to validate its performance. The ultimate goal of our decision problem is to maximize the long-run average reward obtained through implementing an operational statistic. Thus, the validation model is Note thatψ(ΛT,g(X))is theactual rewardobtained, and the expectation of the validation model is taken over the random sampleX. The challenge of solving the validation model lies in the fact that we do not know the exact value ofΛT. We need to analyze the properties of the actual reward functionψ(ΛT,g(X))to derive the optimal operational statistic. Solving the validation model in (9) requires finding a solution in the functional space, which, in general, can be computationally difficult, as the decision itself is infinite dimensional. To facilitate the calculation of the optimal operational statistic, we first examine how the sample space is related to the choice of a decision implied by the data-integration model defined inEquation (8). For any finite sample sizenand a nonnegative real numberm∈R+, define them-base set of samples as In other words, any sample with a sample average ofmbelongs to the setBn(m). Now take an arbitrary samplexwith sample averageη(x)=1n∑i=1nxi. If we scale every element inxby a factor ofm/η(x), we obtain a new vectorz=mx/η(x), and it is clear thatz∈Bn(m). That is, any arbitrary samplexcan be projected to an elementzin them-base setBn(m). Reversely, take an arbitraryz∈Bn(m), we can identify a subset of samplesS(z)={x:x=ηz,∀η>0}such that the transformation of elements inS(z)corresponds toz. Thus, for a fixedm,{S(z):z∈Bn(m)}forms a partition of the sample spaceR+n; that is,∪z∈Bn(m)S(z)=R+nandS(z1)∩S(z2)=∅for anyz1,z2∈Bn(m)withz1≠z2. With this observation, we can derive the following result. Givenh:R+n→Rand any fixedm∈R+, Lemma 2states that the integration of anyn-dimensional (integrable) function can be computed by first integrating the function over a scaling factor and then integrating over them-base set. Based on this lemma, we can evaluate the validation model to identify the optimal operational statistic,μODA(X), without knowing the true value ofΛT. Moreover, the validation model, which looks for an optimal function within the data-integration model, reduces to a single-dimensional optimization problem, as established in the next theorem. (The OptimalM/M/c/k-ODA Solution).The optimal operational statistic that solves(9)is Moreover, The ODA solution is derived inTheorem 1based on the sample-space partition suggested inLemma 2. In particular, we focus on the unit-base set with norm one, that is,Bn(1)(we shall note that the result carries through when we choose a base set with any positive norm). Because an operational statistic is selected fromGdefined inEquation (8), we can infer a solutionμ(x)for any generalxif we know the solutionμ(z)for the corresponding base pointz=(1n∑i=1nxi)−1xwith the relationμ(x)=(1n∑i=1nxi)−1μ(z). It turns out that the optimal solution withinGgives the same value for any base point; that is,μBODAis a constant and is independent of the sample observations. This is a direct consequence of the facts that the reward function is homogenous of degree zero (recallLemma 1), and the unknown interarrival time comes from the exponential family forM/M/c/kqueues. Consequently, the original functional optimization problem boils down to finding the maximizer of a scalar function inEquation (11). Note that the theoretically optimal service rate isμ*(ΛT)=argmax{ψ(ΛT,μ),μ≥0}. The base operational statisticμBODAinEquation (11)is derived without the knowledge ofΛT. Instead,μBODAis optimized from an integral equation involvingψ. In our later discussion of the general case inSection 5, we will see that this integral equation corresponds to the optimal objective prior of the unknownΛTfor the service design problem. Theorem 1derives the optimal operational statistic,μODA, withinGthat maximizes the validation model. Because the service ratesμETOandμETO-Sderived under the estimation-and-then-optimization approach also belong toG, it is clear that the ODA solution dominates the ETO solutions. According toTheorem 1, if the observed sample falls in the unit-base setBn(1), the optimal service rate isμBODA. Otherwise, the optimal service rate is the unit-base service rateμBODAscaled by the sample average. Hence, the optimal service rate depends on the observed samplexonly via the sample average. It is easy to see that the optimal service rateμODA(x)belongs to the scaled class of operational statistics, InFigure 1, we demonstrate how the ODA service speed responds to the sample size. In this example, the true arrival rate isΛT=1. We observe that with small samples, the ODA solutionμODAis apparently closer to the theoretically optimal solution than the ETO solutionμETOis. As the sample size increases, the differences vanish. It is interesting to see that the ODA solution and the ETO solution may deviate from the theoretically optimal solution in different directions. InFigure 2, we plot the percentage optimality gap of the ODA reward against the theoretically optimal reward. We also observe a quickly improved ODA reward as the sample size increases from one. This suggests that the ODA solution can effectively utilize limited data. Moreover, ODA performance improves as the buffer size gets small (i.e.,cis large orkis small). FromFigures 1and2, both the ODA solution and the resulting reward approach their theoretically optimal counterparts as the sample size increases. We can formally establish the consistency of the ODA approach. For that, we first make an observation of the offered load (i.e., the ratio of the arrival rate to the service rate) in the next lemma. LetSn=n/Λ^X=∑i=1nXi,ρETO(Sn)=ΛT/μ*(Λ^X),andρ*=argmax{ψ(ρ,1),ρ>0}.Then, Lemma 3suggests that the difference between the estimated offered loadρETO(Sn)and the theoretically optimal offered loadρ*is bounded. With this observation, we can derive the bound of the ETO reward, which, in turn, gives rise to the bound of the ODA reward. (Consistency of theM/M/c/k-ODA Solution).The reward obtained from implementing the optimal ODA solution is consistent; that is, Ifμ*(ΛT)is unique, the optimal operational statistic is asymptotically optimal; that is, In summary, we see that the data-integratedM/M/c/kproblem reveals some interesting properties. The optimal operational statistic can be computed efficiently by recognizing that the reward function is homogenous of degree zero, which, in turn, leads to the scaling structure of the solution. In the next section, we will extend our analysis to service systems with general arrival processes and general service time distributions.
article.4. Service Speed Design for General Syst: In this section, we generalize the analysis to account for general distributions of interarrival time and service time. Consider a service system withcservers and(k−c)buffers. In reality, the service capacity and the buffer size are almost always limited by the available resources. For example, a production line would utilize up to the available machines for processing and the available space for work-in-progress inventory, a call center would have a certain number of agents and put a certain number of incoming calls on hold, and a store would have limited checkout counters and waiting area. A finiteksuggests that the queuing system is always stable. The interarrival time isZ/ΛT, whereZhas a cumulative distribution functionFZand density functionfZ, andΛTis the true arrival rate. The service time isV/μ, whereVhas a cumulative distribution functionFVand density functionfV, andμis the chosen service rate. Service speed scalability is observed in many practices. In the production environment, the service load can be distributed among available machines or operators, each sharing a portion of the requested service load. For on-demand service platforms, a fundamental aspect of power management design for CPU, switch fabrics, and wireless network is the appropriate speed scaling based on the anticipated workload (e.g.,Andrew et al. 2009). Such a modeling approach has also been adopted in studies of service supply chains (e.g.,So 2000,Borst et al. 2004). For instance, large-scale service systems such as call centers often determine routing policies to multiple-agent pools based on multiple customer requests to achieve the desired service level (e.g.,Gurvich and Whitt 2009,Lu et al. 2009). Service rates may also be scaled by including or excluding add-on services like consultation (Debo et al. 2008) or cross-selling (Byers and So 2007). Letλ(Λ,μ)andW(Λ,μ)denote the effective arrival rate and the expected waiting time, respectively, when the arrival rate is Λ, and the service rate isμ. Because the available space in the system,k, is finite, not all arrivals may enter the system; that is,λ(Λ,μ)≤Λ. The long-run average reward is computed as In a stable system,λ(Λ,μ)=(1/η)λ(ηΛ,ημ)andW(Λ,μ)=ηW(ηΛ,ημ)for anyη>0.Consequently, the long-run average rewardψ(Λ,μ)is a homogenous function of degree zero, that is,ψ(ηΛ,ημ)=ψ(Λ,μ),and the theoretically optimal service rate satisfiesμ*(Λ)=Λμ*(1)for anyη>0. Lemma 4implies that time scaling does not change the long-run average service reward. As it is for theM/M/c/ksystem, this property is essential to develop the ODA solution for the general service system. 4.1. The ODA Service Speed Design To determine a service rate based on the data of interarrival timesx, a natural data-integration model, as discussed inSection 3.2, is A direct validation of the solution leads to the expected optimal actual reward: To optimize the validation model over the functional space, we apply the sample space partition described inSection 3. Specifically, letBn(1)={x:1n∑i=1nxi=1}be the unit-base set, andS(z)={x:x=ηz,∀η>0}be the collection of samples that can be obtained from scaling a given elementzin the unit-base set. Then, with the results inLemmas 2and4, the functional optimization problem of validation boils down to finding an optimal decision for each element within the unit-base set, as suggested by the next theorem. (The Optimal ODA Solution).Let Then, the optimal operational statistic is Moreover, Similar to the problem ofM/M/c/ksystem treated inTheorem 1, the problem ofG/G/c/ksystem can be solved by first finding the optimal operational statistic for the unit-base setBn(1)based onTheorem 3and then scaling the base operational statistic with the sample average. The unit-base operational statisticμBODA(z)can be derived by solving the single-dimensional optimization problem inEquation (14)for every base pointz∈Bn(1). Thus, the original problem of finding an operational statistic over the functional spaceGreduces to the scalar optimization problem inEquation (14). Compared withTheorem 1for theM/M/c/ksystem where the interarrival time is exponentially distributed, the unit-base operational statisticμBODA(z)inTheorem 3is no longer a constant for a generalG/G/c/ksystem. With a generalfZ, the likelihood function∏i=1nfZ(γzi)depends onznot just through its average. Consequently, the solution for the general arrival distribution can no longer be determined solely by the sample average, and thus, the optimal operational statistic is not within the scaled class in general; that is,μODA(x)∉GSC. It is worth noting that gamma density reveals a similar structure as the exponential density. (Gamma Arrivals).WhenfZ(z)=zκ−1κκe−κzΓ(κ),z≥0,the optimal operational statistic is Corollary 1suggests that the ODA solution for gamma interarrival time also belongs to the scaled classGSC. This is because, though the likelihood function∏i=1nfZ(γzi)of the gamma distribution depends onznot only on its average, the derivative of the likelihood function with respect to the scale parameter depends only on the average. Note that the gamma distribution with shape parameterκ= 1 reduces to the exponential distribution. The well-studied Erlang distribution in queuing systems belongs to the gamma family withκbeing some natural number. Because the estimation-and-then-optimization solutionμETO(x)=μ*(Λ^x)is an element of the data-integration modelG, and the ODA solution is uniformly optimal overG, the performance ofμODA(·)always dominates that ofμETO(·). Both solutions achieve asymptotic optimality when the sample size gets large, as established in the next theorem. (Consistency of the ODA Reward).The expected reward obtained by implementing the ODA solutionμODA(·)is consistent. Specifically, 4.2. Applications We demonstrate the applications of the results in the previous subsection to two well-studied queuing systems, one with a general interarrival distribution and the other with a general service time distribution. These applications allow us to isolate the effect of different distributional properties on the performance of the ODA solution. 4.2.1. General Arrival Process. When the interarrival time follows a general distribution, the problem becomes the service design of aG/M/c/ksystem. As a standard treatment, we can define an embedded Markov chain of the customer flow. Let{Xn,n=0,1,2,…}denote the number of customers in the system right before thenth arrival, and let{Yn,n=0,1,2,…}denote the number of departures during thenth interarrival time (i.e., the time between the(n−1)st arrival and thenth arrival). Then, we can explicitly compute the distribution of[Yn|Xn−1](see the details in Online Appendix B.2); that is, Then,{Xn,n=0,1,2,…}is a Markov chain following the dynamics The transition probabilitiespij(Λ,μ)=Pr{Xn+1=j|Xn=i}and the steady-state distributionπi(Λ,μ)=Pr{X∞=i}can also be explicitly calculated, as shown in Online Appendix B.2. With these values, the effective arrival rate to the system is One can easily verify thatνij(λ,μ)=νij(ηΛ,ημ)andπi(Λ,μ)=πi(ηΛ,ημ)so that the reward function inEquation (13)is degree-zero homogeneous. 4.2.2. General Service Time. When the service time follows some nonexponential distribution, the service system becomes anM/G/c/kqueue. The steady-state analysis for generalcdoes not yield explicit forms, and many approaches, including diffusion approximation (e.g.,Whitt 2004), infinite probabilities (e.g.,Tijms 1992), two-moment approximation (e.g.,Smith 2008), and simulation (e.g.,Cruz et al. 2005), have been developed to derive close approximations of various performance measures. These approximations can be used in the computation of the ODA solution. Here, we focus on theM/G/1/kqueue to demonstrate the result. As standard, define{Yn,n=0,1,2,…}as the number of arrivals during thenth service time; then, the distribution ofYnis We can define an embedded Markov chain of the system. Specifically, let{Xn,n=0,1,2,…}denote the number of customers in the system right after thenth departure. This Markov chain follows the dynamics Based on these dynamics, we can derive the transition probabilitiespij(Λ,μ)=Pr{Xn+1=j|Xn=i}and the steady-state distributionπi(Λ,μ)=Pr{X∞=i}. The details can be found in Online Appendix B.3. Thus, the effective arrival rate is One can easily verify thatνj(λ,μ)=νj(ηΛ,ημ)andπi(Λ,μ)=πi(ηΛ,ημ)so that the reward function inEquation (13)is degree-zero homogeneous. 4.3. Small Sample Performance of ODA: Numerical Analysis Though we have established the asymptotic optimality and finite-sample uniform optimality of the ODA solution, it is important to understand the magnitude with which the solution performance differs from the conventional approach. We are particularly interested in the small sample regime as this is the most relevant case in reality. We present the performance of the ODA solution forG/M/c/ksystems, and the result forM/G/c/ksystems is reported in Online Appendix C. InTable 1, the interarrival time follows gamma distributions. In this case, the optimal operational statistics belong to the scaled classGSC.Table 2demonstrates the result for beta interarrival time, for which the optimal solution is not inGSC. As a general observation, the estimation-and-then-optimization solution with the sample-average estimator (ETO) is dominated by that with the Stein’s estimator (ETO-S), which is, in turn, dominated by the ODA solution. Moreover, the ODA solution produces not only the highest average reward but also the lowest standard deviation of the reward and the highest minimum reward. This suggests that the ODA solution is more robust than the ETO and ETO-S solutions in the small sample regime. Table 1.Performance of the ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems with Gamma Distribution Table 1.Performance of the ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems with Gamma Distribution Notes. The interarrival time follows gamma distribution; that is,Z∼Gamma(κ,θ)withκθ=1and true arrival rateΛT=1, and the service time is exponentially distributed. The system has a gross margin ofp−b=1, a waiting cost ofw= 0.3, andc= 4 servers. The table presents the average, standard deviation, and minimum of the rewards under the estimation-and-then-optimization with the sample mean (ETO), estimation-and-then-optimization with the Stein’s estimate (ETO-S), and operational data analytics (ODA) solutions for 5,000 simulated samples. Table 2.Performance of the ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems with Scaled Beta Distribution Table 2.Performance of the ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems with Scaled Beta Distribution Notes. The interarrival time follows scaled beta distribution, that is,Z∼a+ba×Beta(a,b)and true arrival rateΛT=1, and the service time is exponentially distributed. The system has a gross margin ofp−b=1, a waiting cost ofw= 0.3, andc= 4 servers. The table presents the average, standard deviation, and minimum of the rewards under the estimation-and-then-optimization with the sample mean (ETO), estimation-and-then-optimization with the Stein’s estimate (ETO-S), and operational data analytics (ODA) solutions for 5,000 simulated samples. The inherent uncertainty in the systems is partly reflected by the coefficient of variations (i.e.,Cv[Z])of interarrival time. The larger the coefficient of variation, the higher the variability in the arrival process is. A large buffer size (k) also induces a higher uncertainty in the system as the largest potential queue size (i.e., congestion level) increases withk. When the variability of the process increases (because of an increasedCv[Z]ork), the theoretically optimal reward, the rewards under ETO and ETO-S approaches, and the reward under the ODA approach all reduce. Compared with the ETO and ETO-S approaches, the ODA approach produces a higher average reward. Especially when the sample size is small, the difference in the average rewards is very significant. When the level of uncertainty is high (with a highCv[Z]or a largek), a large sample is needed to provide sufficient information to reflect the system dynamics. We observe that the difference of the average rewards under the ETO or ETO-S solution from that under the ODA solution becomes small fork= 10 andCv[Z]≤1. However, the ODA solution still shows a clear advantage in either the standard deviation or the minimum of the reward. We also observe that the reward under the ETO or ETO-S solution is more sensitive to the buffer size (reflected byk) than that under the ODA solution. That is, the ETO approach produces significantly worse performance whenkis larger, whereas the reward obtained from the ODA approach is relatively stable.
article.5. The ODA Prediction-and-Then-Optimizat: In this section, we present an alternative view of the ODA framework developed in the previous section. This viewpoint recovers an updated distribution of the unknown arrival process based on the homogeneous property of the data-integration model. The updated distribution allows us to draw inferences of the performance measures, possibly combined with additional knowledge and experience. This viewpoint allows us to examine other potential design decisions in addition to the service speed. As our discussion unfolds later, it becomes clear that the ODA prediction-and-then-optimization approach is different from the existing estimation-and-then-optimization methods. The latter derives estimates based on statistical or machine learning approaches and then uses the estimates to replace the true parameters in computing the objective. Our approach, in contrast, utilizes the inherent structure of the data-integrated service design problem to derive a prediction for the reward function. 5.1. ODA Prediction of the Reward An alternative view of the ODA framework is to dissect the information flow embedded in the data-integration model and use the information implied by the data to obtain an estimate of the actual reward under any given sample size. Specifically, because we know that the reward functionψis homogenous of order zero, we can dismantle the underlying information updating mechanism implied by the operational statistic to obtain an estimate of the reward function. (The Implied Objective Prior).For anyg∈Gdefined in(8), Theorem 5has two important implications. First, the ODA approach that applies some operational statistic withinGdefined inEquation (8)corresponds to the approach of Bayesian estimation and then optimization. In particular, if we assume that the unknown parameter Λ has priorfΛ(γ)=1/γ, thenfΛODA(·|x)is the posterior density given the observed samplex. In other words, choosing a decision within the data-integration modelGis equivalent to estimating the arrival rate with Bayesian priorfΛ(·). Then, we derive the prediction of the reward functionϕODAbased on the posterior of the unknown parameter. This means that foreveryobserved sample, the optimal Bayesian solution with the priorfΛcoincides with the optimal ODA solution. We should remark thatfΛis termed animproper priorin the Bayesian literature, as it does not integrate to one. However, it leads to an appropriately defined posterior that integrates to one. This prior is an uninformative prior for the scale parameter of a distribution (Berger 2013). Second, the functionϕODA(·,c,k|x)constitutes a natural estimation of the reward functionψ(ΛT,·)based on the observed datax. It is important to note thatϕODA(·,c,k|x)is a function of the dataxand is independent of the unknownΛT. The optimal operational statistic for the observed dataxcan be derived by maximizing the estimated rewardϕODA(·,c,k|x). In other words, the ODA solution derived inSection 4can be alternatively derived through optimizing the ODA-predicted rewardϕODA(·,c,k|x). This is the ODA prediction-and-then-optimization view. (The ODA Reward ofM/G/c/kSystems).When the interarrival time follows exponential distribution, for anygν(x)=ν(1n∑i=1nxi)−1∈GSC,ν∈R+, When the arrival process is Poisson, the optimal operational statistic belongs to the scaled classGSC(recallTheorem 1). In this case, the predicted reward under the ODA approachϕODAbecomes independent of the observed samplex. In other words, regardless of the observed sample, the estimated optimal reward (with respect to the optimal operational statistic) is a constantϕ¯n, though the optimal operational statisticμODAand the implied posteriorfΛODAboth depend on the sample. This is an interesting observation, indicating the robustness of the estimated reward regardless of the sample uncertainty. We shall remark that, even though the operational statistic for gamma arrival belongs to the scaled class (recallCorollary 1), the ODA-predicted reward generally depends on the individual sample observation inx. Thus,Corollary 2holds only for exponential distribution and cannot be extended to gamma distribution. InTable 3, we demonstrate the accuracy of the ODA-predicted rewardϕODA(·,c,k|x)forG/M/c/ksystems (the result forM/G/c/ksystems is similar and can be found in Online Appendix C). We use the squared deviation from the theoretical rewardψ(ΛT,·)to measure the prediction accuracy. It is clear fromTable 3that compared with the reward computed based on the estimated arrival rate,ϕES(·,c,k|x)defined in (6), the ODA-predicted reward has a significantly smaller error. This suggests that using the ODA-predicted reward to determine service parameters can produce better decisions than computing the reward using the estimated arrival rate. Table 3.ODA Reward Estimate forG/M/c/kSystems Table 3.ODA Reward Estimate forG/M/c/kSystems Notes. The interarrival time follows gamma distribution; that is,Z∼Gamma(κ,θ)withκθ=1and true arrival rateΛT=1, and the service time is exponentially distributed. The system has a gross margin ofp−b=1, a waiting cost ofw= 0.3, andc= 4 servers. The candidate service rates{μℓ:ℓ=0,1,…,10}are evenly chosen from[0.5μ*,1.5μ*], whereμ*is the theoretical optimal service rate. The distance is evaluated by the squared deviation from the theoretical reward, that is,dM(X)=111∑ℓ=010|ϕM(μ,c,k|x)−ψ(ΛT,μℓ)|2,M∈{ES,ODA}, where ES and ODA represent the rewards computed under the sample-mean estimate and under the ODA estimate. The table presents the average, standard deviation, and maximum of deviation measures for 5,000 simulated samples. Taking the viewpoint of the objective prior, a natural question is whetherfΛ(γ)=1/γis the best prior for our service design problem under the Bayesian approach. To answer this question, suppose we are given a prior That is, the posterior derived withf0as the prior always has a proper density. Define the reward function computed from the resulting posterior as The next theorem establishes thatfΛ(γ)=1/γis the optimal prior that achieves the minimum regret measured by the competitive ratio. (Bayesian View of ODA Solution).For anyf0∈F,and vectorZof n i.i.d. draws of fZ, Theorem 6also provides the theoretical justification for the ODA solution derived inSection 4. In particular, deriving the ODA solution is equivalent to identifying the optimal prior for the objective function. The advantage of the ODA approach lies in the fact that the predicted rewardϕODAis derived based on the structural property of the service design problem. In particular, the operational statistics withinGcapture the interaction between the arrival process and the service process. In an essential contrast, the estimated parameters derived using the conventional statistical methods are intended to optimize certain loss functions (e.g., squared deviations) and to retain the unbiasedness, which may not be in line with the goal of the service design. In the next theorem, we formally establish the consistency of the ODA-predicted reward. This result is a direct consequence of the consistency of the Bayesian estimate. (The ODA-Predicted Reward).Suppose the following conditions hold: (i)fZ/Λ(·|Λ)is twice differentiable with respect to Λ, and the derivatives are bounded. (ii)fZ/Λ(·|Λ)>0for any Λ within the neighborhood ofΛT. (iii)∂fZ/Λ/∂Λand∂2fZ/Λ/∂Λ2exist and are continuous in Λ around the neighborhood ofΛT. (iv)ψ(Λ,μ)is bounded for any fixedμ>0. The reward functionϕODAis a consistent estimate of the true reward function; that is, The proof ofTheorem 7relies on the consistency of the Bayesian posterior for the uninformative prior. In particular, under the standard regularity conditions (i)–(iii), the maximum-likelihood estimate converges to a normal random variable with the mean equal to the true mean and the standard deviation proportional to1/n. Moreover, under the uninformative prior, the Bayesian posterior converges to a normal random variable with the mean equal to the maximum-likelihood estimate and the standard deviation proportional to1/n. This result, along with the boundedness and smoothness of the reward function, implies the consistency of the ODA-predicted reward. 5.2. Prediction with Additional Knowledge of the Arrival Process In many situations, we may have additional knowledge of the arrival process based on past experience and believe that the true arrival rateΛTis within some setU⊆R+. For example, given our understanding of the market potential, we would not expect the arrival rateΛTto go beyond a certain level, say,λh. Likewise, there may be a minimum levelλlthat the true arrival rate cannot go below. In this case, our knowledge can be described asΛT∈U=[λl,λh]. In general,Ucan be any subset ofR+. With the knowledge ofU, we can apply the Bayesian mechanism inTheorem 5to derive the implied distribution of the arrival rate as In other words, we utilize the dataxto update the objective priorfΛ(γ)∝1/γdefined over the supportγ∈U. The consistency of the posteriorf^ΛODA(γ|x)can be established similarly when the true parameter is within the knowledge set, that is,ΛT∈U(Diaconis and Freedman 1986). Replacing the updated distribution inTheorem 5with that in (21), we can then compute the estimated reward functionϕ^ODA(μ,c,k|x). This estimated reward can be optimized with respect to key design parameters of the service system. InTable 4, we present the performance of the ODA service rate decision under different knowledge of the arrival rate forG/M/c/ksystems (the result forM/G/c/ksystems is presented in Table 9 in the Online Appendix). In these experiments, we assume that the true arrival rateΛTlies in the middle of the intervalUwith length2δ. Whenδgets smaller, we are more sure of the arrival rate. When computing the ODA solution, we use the posterior distribution in (21) to account for the knowledgeU. For the ETO benchmark, we use the constrained estimate (i.e.,min{L(Λ^,X)=1n∑i=1n(Λ^−Xi)2:Λ^∈U}). It is well-known that the constrained estimator may be biased. FromTable 4, it is clear that when we are more sure about where the trueΛTlies (i.e., whenδis smaller), the performance of both ETO and ODA solutions becomes better, and the difference between the two solutions gets smaller. It is important to note that the ODA solution is much less sensitive toδthan the ETO solution. In other words, the ODA solution is robust to the range of the unknown parameter, which determines the model specification. As long as we have some rough knowledge of where the true arrival rate is, optimizing the ODA-predicted reward can lead to efficient decisions. This observation echoes the conclusion byFeng et al. (2022), who find that the ODA-predicted choice model can best utilize rough but imprecise knowledge of the underlying system. Our analysis here takes one step further—the ODA approach not only generates an accurate prediction of the underlying model but also produces good decision quality. Table 4.Effect of Arrival Knowledge on the Performance of ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems Table 4.Effect of Arrival Knowledge on the Performance of ODA Solution forG/M/c/kSystems Notes. The interarrival time follows gamma distribution, i.e.,Z∼Gamma(κ,θ)withκθ=1and true arrival rateΛT=1, and the service time is exponentially distributed. The arrival knowledge specifiesU=[ΛT−δ,ΛT+δ]. The system has a gross margin ofp−b=1, a waiting cost ofw= 0.3,c= 4 servers, andk= 20 spaces. The table presents the average, standard deviation, and minimum of the rewards under the estimation-and-then-optimization with the sample mean (ETO) and operational data analytics (ODA) solutions for 5,000 simulated samples. 5.3. Discrete Additions of Service Speed With the ODA-predicted reward, we can examine other design parameters of the service system. In addition to the service rate, the speed of service can also be adjusted by changing the number of serversc. In some contexts, the productivity of individual servers may be predetermined. For example, a call center may conduct a standard training program so that agents follow a standard call response process. A manufacturing system may fine-tune the machinery so that processing follows uniform routines. Implementation of these protocols allows the decision maker to have a good understanding of the service time of each resource. To match the service speed with the customer demand, the major lever is the service capacityc, which is determined by the scheduling of equipment and staffing level. In customer-intensive systems where customer arrival exhibits heavy traffic patterns, one may solve for the optimal system service rate of the system and scale it by the single-server service rate to find the approximate number of servers (Borst et al. 2004). Such an approach, however, may not work well in a light traffic regime. Moreover, when only limited arrival data are available, one may not be able to judge the intensity of the arrival traffic. We, instead, look for the number of servers that maximizes the ODA-predicted reward Table 5.ODA Capacity Decision forG/M/c/kSystems Table 5.ODA Capacity Decision forG/M/c/kSystems Notes. The interarrival time follows gamma distribution, that is,Z∼Gamma(κ,θ)withκθ=1and true arrival rateΛT, and the service time is exponentially distributed with rateμ=0.3. The system has a gross margin ofp−b=1, a waiting cost ofw= 0.3, andk= 20 spaces. The optimal number of servers is computed ascM=argmax{ϕM(μ,c,k|x),c∈N},M∈{ETO,ETO-S,ODA}. The table presents the average, standard deviation, and minimum of the rewards under the estimation-and-then-optimization with the sample mean (ETO), estimation-and-then-optimization with the Stein’s estimate (ETO-S), and operational data analytics (ODA) solutions for 5,000 simulated samples.
article.6. Concluding Remarks: In this study, we analyze data-integrated service design forG/G/c/ksystems. By exploring the relationship between the service rate and the arrival data and the homogeneous property of the key service performance measures (e.g., joining probability, queue length, and steady-state probability), we formulate the data-integration model as a class of operational statistics and identify the optimal service rate that validates the eventual long-run service reward. Taking the Bayesian view of the ODA framework, we derive an ODA-predicted reward function, which closely estimates the underlying reward function. We prove the consistency of the ODA solution and reward and demonstrate the superior performance of the ODA approach under the small sample regime. Most importantly, we observe that the ODA solution is robust to inherent uncertainties in the service process and the knowledge of the unknown parameter. This work is the first step toward a direct data-to-decision solution for multiserver queues. As theG/G/c/kqueue has been widely applied in many contexts, the uniformly optimal ODA solution may constitute a major building block to studying data-driven decision making in, for example, service rate–dependent quality (Anand et al. 2011), decentralized admission control (Gilbert and Weng 1998), and strategic server response (Geng et al. 2015,Gopalakrishnan et al. 2016,Armony et al. 2021,Wang et al. 2022). Many of these settings would require introducing additional endogenous variables into the data-integration model to enrich the structure of the operational statistics. In reality, structural knowledge of the system may be imprecise. Analyzing the effect of model misspecification (e.g.,Hu et al. 2022) on the decision performance is an important direction for future research. One would need to develop ODA solutions to appropriately correct the imprecise structural knowledge with the available data (e.g.,Feng et al. 2022). Further development of ODA solutions to accommodate dynamic learning that balances exploitation and exploration (Hsu et al. 2022) is another important avenue for future research. The existing learning approaches (e.g., bandit algorithm, upper confidence bounds, and episodic design) often separate learning and earning. As a major departure from this approach, dynamic ODA learning would require a specification of how the decision should be dynamically dependent on data collected over time. Specifically, in the context of service design, one needs to appropriately control the transient process with the available data before stabilizing the system performance.

AItitle: 排队系统决策新突破：ODA框架实现小样本均匀最优解
AIsummary: 该论文研究排队系统中的服务速度设计问题，聚焦于多服务器有限容量的G/G/c/k模型，旨在解决传统方法在小样本数据下服务速率决策不精准的难题。以往研究依赖“先估计后优化”（ETO）框架，通过历史数据估算参数（如到达率）再求解最优解，但数据不足时存在显著偏差。作者提出“操作数据分析”（ODA）框架，绕过参数估计，直接建立数据到决策的映射：基于服务过程的时标特性，构建同质化数据整合模型，将服务速率设计为样本数据的函数，并通过验证模型计算长期平均奖励来确保决策的均匀最优性。实验表明，ODA在小样本下显著优于传统ETO方法，且对模型设定不敏感。此外，作者扩展了贝叶斯视角，证明ODA可视为基于无信息先验的参数更新，并推导出奖励函数的一致估计，为服务容量等扩展设计提供高效数据驱动方案。
Highlights:
学术要点 1：时间缩放属性（Time-Scaling Property） 排队系统的稳态分布和服务度量在时间维度缩放时保持不变，允许通过齐次函数建立服务速率与到达数据的直接映射关系。|||
学术要点 2：操作统计量（Operational Statistic） 基于样本数据构造的同质函数映射规则，无需参数估计即可直接生成服务速率决策，突破传统"先估计后优化"范式。|||
学术要点 3：均匀最优性（Uniform Optimality） 通过验证模型推导的服务速率解在任意样本量下保持最优性，其最优性间隙受1/n量级约束。|||
学术要点 4：贝叶斯客观先验（Objective Bayesian Prior） 采用1/γ形式的非信息先验，将操作统计量等价转化为贝叶斯后验更新过程，实现奖励函数的一致性估计。|||
学术要点 5：小样本机制（Small Sample Regime） 在有限数据场景下，ODA框架通过结构保持特性降低采样风险，其预测奖励的均方误差显著低于传统估计方法。
AIfig: 该图展示了基于历史到达数据的ODA框架与传统优化方法在不同样本量下的服务奖励对比。
图片展示了ODA框架与传统估计优化方法在小样本下的服务速率设计对比结果。
